topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my afternoon. My stomach clenched, a familiar knot tightening beneath my ribs. It was a request. From Mark. The name alone was enough to send a spike of icy resentment through me. I stared at the profile picture—a poorly lit, overly confident grin from a decade ago—and felt a slow burn of irritation rise in my chest. I didn't even need to click it. I just wanted to delete the app, to erase the digital ghost of that unpleasantness. My fingers hovered over the 'ignore' button, but a strange resistance kept me rooted. I took a deep, shaky breath, the air tasting stale. I knew exactly what this move was designed to do: pull me back into a cycle I’d worked hard to break free from. I closed the messaging app without responding, a silent declaration that I wasn't interested in reopening any old wounds.

[story 2]

I saw the notification pop up, and immediately, my jaw tightened. It was him. That same face, radiating that familiar, unwelcome swagger. A low, simmering anger began to bubble up, hot and insistent. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, a physical barrier against whatever intrusion this was. Why now? After all this time, he thought he could just slide back into my orbit? A wry, almost mocking smile touched my lips, though it felt brittle. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the invitation. The impulse to confront him, to deliver a sharp, cutting remark, was strong. But I held back. I watched the screen, letting the tension coil in my shoulders, refusing to give him the satisfaction of seeing me react. I didn't reply. I just let the silence stretch, heavy and deliberate.

[story 3]

When the alert flashed, I felt an immediate surge of defensive energy. My hands instinctively clenched into fists on the desk, the knuckles turning white. It was him. The memory of his sneering face flashed behind my eyes, and a wave of profound annoyance washed over me. I didn't bother reading the details of the request; I just felt the need to establish distance. My internal monologue was sharp, calculating: *Why is he doing this? What does he want?* I deliberately avoided touching the notification, letting the digital summons sit there, an irritating pebble in my otherwise smooth day. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between my desk and the window. The restlessness in my legs was a clear signal that I wasn't going to be swayed by this. I walked to the kitchen, poured myself a glass of water, and took a slow, measured sip, trying to settle the frantic energy thrumming beneath my skin.
